quote: Research showing the impact of varying stand density and canopy cover on fuel loads and understory biomass would be beneficial, as this would help inform producers of the optimal balance between tree density, forage production, and livestock stocking that match site capability and their desired management goals.
attribution: Batcheler et al. 2024

relationship.qualifier: dryland forest (ponderosa pine dominant); Köppen Csb warm-summer Mediterranean; private non-industrial forest

quote: the sites are limited to dryland forest habitats in the Interior Columbia Basin
attribution: Batcheler et al. 2024

quote: Land tenure security was determined to be major factor in the negative effects of this program since farmers in the region were reluctant to invest the time and resources in the management the agroforestry systems when they did not have tenure security.
attribution: Dai et al. (2017) crop-fruit tree intercropping program, rural Xinjiang, China — assessed within Castle et al. (2021) systematic review.

quote: They also found a perceived increase in tenure security because of the HKm program of 26.4% (SE: 2.0).
attribution: Pender et al. (2008) HKm social forestry permitting program, Sumatra, Indonesia — assessed within Castle et al. (2021) systematic review.

lineage_of: Lawry et al. (2014) systematic review, Campbell Systematic Reviews
quote: land tenure interventions are plausible pathways to improve welfare.
attribution: Lawry et al. (2014) cited in Castle et al. (2021) p.29: limited evidence base (n=20), general finding on tenure interventions and agricultural productivity.

quote: even with these direct offsets, an increased labor requirement, increased difficulty in farm management, or lack of market access may still deter farmers from adopting the promoted agroforestry practice(s).
attribution: Synthesised finding across incentive-pathway intervention studies in Castle et al. (2021) p.27; covers Haggar et al. 2017 (Nicaragua), Hegde & Bull 2011 (Mozambique), Pagiola et al. 2016 (Colombia), Pender et al. 2008 (Indonesia).

quote: provision of information, raw materials, and technical support, which includes support for activities like extension and training programs and germplasm provision.
attribution: Pathway 1 synthesis across Bostedt et al. 2016, Coulibaly et al. 2017, Kuntashula & Mungatana 2013, Thorlakson & Neufeldt 2012, Dai et al. 2017, Sills & Caviglia-Harris 2015, Place et al. 2005 — in Castle et al. (2021) p.26.

quote: Coulibaly et al. (2017) explicitly analyzed the impact of the agroforestry program on adoption of agroforestry practices, and they found that receiving agroforestry training significantly increased the likelihood of adopting agroforestry practices through building farmer capacity to appropriately manage the trees.
attribution: Coulibaly et al. (2017) fertilizer-tree agroforestry project, Malawi (ICRAF), n=338; cited in Castle et al. (2021) p.26.

quote: three key elements, participant engagement, program exposure, and indirect financial support, lead to agroforestry adoption
attribution: Pathway 1 synthesis in Castle et al. (2021) p.26; covers multiple Sub-Saharan Africa and Asia studies.

quote: even with these direct offsets, an increased labor requirement, increased difficulty in farm management, or lack of market access may still deter farmers from adopting the promoted agroforestry practice(s).
attribution: Synthesised finding across incentive-pathway intervention studies in Castle et al. (2021) p.27; covers Haggar et al. 2017, Hegde & Bull 2011, Pagiola et al. 2016, Pender et al. 2008.

relationship.note: Moderate hillslope range for water quality reforestation scenario. Practice is forest reforestation; slope is technology-driven so threshold cautiously transferable to F2 regeneration-based AF on hillslopes.

quote: hillslopes of 20–35% or ﬂoodplains. These locations are a focus of local reforestation efforts (e.g., the Nature Conservancy/American Forest Foundation Mississippi Delta Floodplain Restoration Program) due to their low potential for land use change conﬂict, high water quality beneﬁts (e.g., nutrient retention and soil stabilization42), and contribution to climate adaptation (e.g., ﬂood mitigation43,44).
attribution: Table 1 Scenario 6 (Water quality). Forest reforestation context; agroforestry regeneration family relevance flagged.

relationship.note: Areas with ≥60% potential tree cover are existing forest, excluded from new tree planting. Implies AF establishment opportunity spans open to semi-open canopy conditions below this threshold.

quote: we deﬁned forests much more conservatively, as areas that can support 60% or more tree cover, to exclude open woodlands and savannas
attribution: Forest potential definition combining Bastin et al. 2019 and Walker et al. 2022. Reforestation mapping context; threshold marks upper boundary of AF new-establishment opportunity.

relationship.note: Secure land tenure is an enabling condition for tree planting. Insecure tenure caused the greatest constrained area reduction among social-conflict scenarios (195 Mha to 116 Mha; 40% reduction). Directional claim; no numeric suitability threshold.

quote: Formally recognized land rights and tenure have been identiﬁed as enabling conditions for successful reforestation
attribution: Table 1 Scenario 3 (Secure land tenure). Cites Rakotonarivo et al. 2023. Reforestation context; operational_constraint enabler transferable to agroforestry.

relationship.note: Areas within 1-h walk of highly deprived communities are high-conflict zones for reforestation. For agroforestry the proximity criterion inverts: near-community locations are primary livelihood AF opportunities requiring conflict-sensitive design rather than exclusion.

quote: excluding areas within a 1-h walk from communities with high deprivation, where reforestation may conﬂict with the livelihoods of impoverished rural populations with high nature-dependence for food and fuel
raw_name: 1-h walk from communities with high deprivation
attribution: Table 1 Scenario 4 (Low rural livelihood conflict). Cites Schultz et al. 2022, Fleischman et al. 2022. Reforestation exclusion reinterpreted as AF social-safeguard proximity flag.

relationship.note: agricultural land eligible for TCA defined as <25% baseline tree cover; above this counted as forestry

quote: we used 25% tree cover as a cutoff in order to avoid any double counting of natural climate solution potential with forestry-based solutions
attribution: Potapov et al. 2022 (cropland layer); forest cover datasets

relationship.note: Delphi-elicited mean maximum tree cover across all biomes and agricultural systems without reducing yields; SD=16%

quote: The mean estimation across all biomes and agricultural systems was 27% with a relatively large standard deviation of 16%.
attribution: Delphi expert elicitation across 53 regional cropping and grazing system categories

relationship.note: Delphi-elicited maximum in forested biomes; paper notes this approaches shade-agroforestry level — caution warranted for shade-intolerant crops

quote: The mean estimation across forested biomes was around 35%, a level that is sometimes considered shade-agroforestry, so these estimations must be applied with caution and close monitoring of agricultural yield impacts on shade-intolerant crops.
attribution: Delphi expert elicitation

relationship.note: Range across forest biomes for cropland; Moist Broadleaf=21%, Dry Broadleaf=42%; Delphi expert elicitation

quote: The mean estimated maximum in croplands in Temperate and Tropical/Subtropical Forest biomes range from 21% in Moist Broadleaf Forests up to 42% for cropland in Dry Broadleaf Forests.
attribution: Delphi expert elicitation, Temperate and Tropical/Subtropical Forest biomes

relationship.note: Mean tree cover increase from baseline to expert-estimated maximum in tropical/subtropical Grasslands, Savannas & Shrublands (grazing lands); delta not absolute maximum

quote: tropical/subtropical GSS comprising more than half of the global potential at 0.81 Pg CO2 yr− 1 over the largest number of hectares (543 M), with a mean tree cover increase of 18% (Table 3).
attribution: Delphi expert elicitation, Table 3

quote: A significant share of Ethiopia's agricultural soils is not as productive as they could be due to a suboptimal pH level. According to Ethiopian Soil Information System data, 28 percent of soils are acidic while a significant proportion are alkaline.
attribution: Ethiopia country context, citing Ethiopian Soil Information System

quote: the extensive loss of soil organic matter is diminishing the productive capacity of Ethiopia's agricultural land and reducing soils' capacity to retain moisture, leaving rainfed farms more vulnerable to drought.
attribution: Ethiopia country context

quote: According to SIGI, the legal framework for land tenure is relatively gender equal, but customary law and traditional practices often supersede it, and enforcement is weak. As a result, 85 percent of land is held in men's names, which perpetuates gender inequalities that affect women's access to productive resources, credit,
attribution: Madagascar country context, citing OECD SIGI 2019

quote: Ethiopia: Fragility and conflict situations in the country; weak PFM and internal audit oversight capacities and weaker infrastructure and communication technology
attribution: FM Risk Table A7.1, country-level inherent risk rating H (High)

quote: The subcomponent will conduct participatory appraisals of the current agricultural extension system to identify its structural weaknesses, understand the roles and links of different stakeholders, and identify (and prioritize) ways in which extension services can reduce farmers' vulnerability, thus increasing the system's capacity to support farmer resilience.
attribution: Ethiopia FSRP Sub-component on extension system strengthening

quote: Women constitute over 70 percent of the agricultural labor force but hold less than 5 percent of agricultural land titles, although weak land rights reduce incentives to invest in land and its productivity.

The smallholder mixed crop-livestock system is found in areas that receive an average annual rainfall of more than 1,000 mm (high-rainfall zones or non-ASAL areas), spreading from central Kenya through the central Rift Valley to western Kenya and the coastal strip.

The crop-livestock-tree production (agro-silvo-pastoral) system is most prevalent in areas that receive an average annual rainfall of 750–1,000 mm (medium rainfall zones or semi-arid areas). This system focuses on the integration of livestock and crops, soil and water conservation, and the production of drought-tolerant and early maturing crops.

The pastoral/extensive livestock production system38 is most common in areas where average annual rainfall is 200–750 mm (low-rainfall zones or arid areas), stretching from northern and northeastern Kenya to the southern areas bordering Tanzania.
Windbreaks, hedgerows, enhanced clearing, live-hedge establishment … Promotion of non-timber forest products … Creating agro-forestry parks … Nursery (village or individual)
contracting private advisory service providers to facilitate mobilization of community institutions and assist in Micro-project planning and implementation

Matching grants will be provided through three windows: (i) community-level investments to finance microprojects (Agro-Pastoral Micro-projects); (ii) county-level investments to finance relatively larger subprojects that benefit multiple wards or communities (Agro-Pastoral Subprojects); and (iii) productive alliance investments through PPPs with producers (PPP Subprojects). Beneficiaries must contribute at least 10 percent of the cost of their microprojects.

Value chain approach:40 An approach that focuses on developing priority agricultural, livestock, and fisheries commodities in the respective counties through interventions covering production, value addition, and links to markets will be taken. This approach will look at supply chains, delivery channels, and enabling environment issues to identify and address bottlenecks and leverage points in the value chains.

relationship.note: 10% tree cover = FAO forest definition; 43% of global agricultural land exceeds this

quote: in 2010, 43% of all agricultural land globally had at least 10% tree cover and that this has increased by 2% over the previous ten years
attribution: Zomer et al. 2014 primary analysis; tree cover data from MOD44B MODIS VCF Collection 5

relationship.note: observed distribution of tree cover on agricultural land by bioclimatic zone; high = humid tropics, low = arid/semi-arid

quote: High tree cover (>​45%) was found in the humid regions such as Southeast Asia, Central America, eastern South America and central and coastal West Africa. Tree cover was moderate (10–30%) in the majority of agricultural areas in South Asia, sub-humid Africa, Central and Western Europe, Amazonian South America, and Midwestern North America. At the other extreme are agricultural areas with low (<​10%) tree cover such as Eastern China, Northwest India and the Punjab, Western Asia, the southern border of the Sahara, the northern prairies of North America and Southwest Australia.
attribution: Primary remote-sensing analysis using MOD44B MODIS VCF 250m, GLC2000

relationship.note: biomass carbon increases linearly with % tree cover; supports T4 tree_canopy_cover response function

quote: Biomass carbon is equal to the default Tier 1 value for agricultural land (5 tC/ha) when there are no trees on that land, ◦ (i.e. tree cover =​ 0%). There is an incremental linear increase of tC/ha proportionally as tree cover increases from the baseline (5 tC/ha at 0% tree cover) up to the maximum value for Mixed Forest in that specific carbon zone
attribution: IPCC Tier 1 GPG method (Ruesch & Gibbs 2008); Zomer et al. modelled extension

relationship.note: three GLC2000 classes define the agricultural land eligibility mask; Cropland/Tree Cover Mosaic listed on p.10

quote: Three agricultural land use types from the Global Land Cover 2000 database were included in our "Agricultural Land" class: • Cultivated and Managed Areas (agriculture — intensive), • Cropland/Other Natural Vegetation (non-trees: mosaic agriculture/degraded vegetation).

relationship.note: global agricultural land extent used as study domain; scale reference for suitability analysis

quote: Overall the amount of area classed as agricultural is ~22.2 million km2 (GLC2000).

relationship.note: aridity is the primary bioclimatic driver of tree cover on agricultural land; humid (low aridity) = high cover; arid = low cover

quote: The distribution of tree cover on agricultural land broadly followed bioclimatic zones – in particular aridity. High tree cover (>​45%) was found in the humid regions such as Southeast Asia, Central America, eastern South America and central and coastal West Africa.

relationship.note: aridity negatively associated with tree cover on agricultural land; humid regions = higher biomass carbon

quote: In summary, both tree cover and consequently biomass carbon on agricultural land tend to be higher in humid regions.

relationship.qualifier: silvopasture reduces grass biomass, litter, and duff depth, thereby reducing fuel loads and wildfire risk; measured at two Washington State ponderosa pine sites

quote: silvopasture management results in reductions in grass biomass, litter, and duff depth when compared to non-grazed managed forest. These findings point to the integrated nature of silvopasture, where management of overstory composition and structure, understory vegetation, and grazing can reduce fuel loads and potential wildfire risk.
raw_name: fuel loads and potential wildfire risk
attribution: Temperate-US scope (Washington State dryland ponderosa pine forest); Mediterranean studies referenced but not the primary site. Findings may not transfer directly to tropical/subtropical or humid-temperate silvopastoral systems.

relationship.qualifier: reduced overstory density and increased spatial variability reduces fire frequency vs other land uses

quote: The reduced density and increased spatial variability of the overstory, combined with other silvopasture management practices, has the potential of reducing fire frequency when compared to other land uses.
attribution: Temperate-US paper; claim cites Mediterranean/other research (ref 32). Mechanism is overstory structure management.

relationship.qualifier: rotational grazing removes understory biomass, reducing herbaceous surface fuel loads

quote: For livestock, use of rotational grazing in a silvopasture has been found to effectively remove understory biomass, thus reducing herbaceous surface fuel loads.
attribution: Temperate-US paper; claim cites Mediterranean silvopasture research (refs 28, 33). Mechanism: rotational grazing forage consumption.

relationship.qualifier: litter and duff depth significantly lower in silvopasture vs non-grazed managed forest at both Washington State study sites (p<=0.05)

quote: Litter and duff depth were significantly lower at both silvopasture sites than the non-grazed managed forests (Table 4). One contributing factor may be due to the significant reduction of grass biomass from livestock grazing at silvopasture sites when compared to non-grazed managed forest sites (Table 5).
attribution: Primary measured data from two Washington State ponderosa pine silvopasture sites; temperate-US scope. Statistical significance at p<=0.05.

relationship.qualifier: litter and duff depth is positively correlated with fire spread rate and burn severity; reducing litter/duff therefore reduces fire spread and severity

quote: Reducing litter and duff has important implications for fire management. There is a correlation between rate of fire spread and burn severity with depth of litter and duff, particularly in this forest type.
raw_name: rate of fire spread and burn severity
attribution: Temperate-US paper (dryland ponderosa pine, Washington State); cite ref 51. Mechanism links litter/duff depth to fire behaviour.

relationship.qualifier: silvopasture reduces litter, duff, and herbaceous biomass (select fuel types) but not coarse woody debris (1h-1000h fuels) vs managed forest; Washington State, Interior Columbia Basin

quote: Our research offers evidence that silvopasture reduces select fuel types and select vegetative biomass when compared to managed forests of the Interior Columbia Basin. However, 1-, 10-, 100-, and 1000-h fuels were similar between the management systems.
raw_name: select fuel types and select vegetative biomass
attribution: Primary measured data from Washington State ponderosa pine sites (Interior Columbia Basin); temperate-US scope. Important nuance: coarse woody fuels (1h-1000h) NOT significantly reduced.

quote: Because of the more deliberate and systems approach to management, silvopastures are capable of improving ecosystem services, such as carbon storage, nutrient cycling, water infiltration rates, and improved biodiversity.
attribution: Temperate-US paper; claim cites ref 26 (Smith et al. 2022 systematic review). Not quantified in this study; listed as ecosystem service of silvopasture generally. Low confidence - no primary data for this variable in this paper.

quote: Adopting agroforestry has been proposed as one livelihood activity that can help build livelihood resilience to floods and droughts (Kandji et al. 2006, Verchot et al. 2007, Garrity et al. 2010, Lin 2011, Thorlakson and Neufeldt 2012, Bryan et al 2013, Simelton et al. 2015).
attribution: Kandji et al. 2006; Verchot et al. 2007; Garrity et al. 2010; Lin 2011; Thorlakson and Neufeldt 2012; Bryan et al 2013; Simelton et al. 2015

context.mitigation_mechanism: deep root systems access greater soil volume for water and nutrients

quote: Some of the ecological characteristics of agroforestry species make them resilient to floods and droughts, including deep root systems that are able to use a greater soil volume for water and nutrients (Kandji et al. 2006, Verchot et al. 2007).
attribution: Kandji et al. 2006; Verchot et al. 2007

context.mitigation_mechanism: shade trees produce microclimates that buffer temperature fluctuations, reducing evapotranspiration

quote: Shade trees can produce microclimates that buffer temperature fluctuations (Lin 2007), which in turn can reduce evapotranspiration.
attribution: Lin 2007

quote: Further, trees have the ability to buffer crops from storms (Philpott et al. 2008).
attribution: Philpott et al. 2008

context.note: Perception-based measure; effect not found in Kinna

quote: for Burat there was a correlation between practicing agroforestry and perception of drought (χ2 = 13.7896, P < 0.01). Households practicing agroforestry largely responded that droughts were less frequent (64 respondents), whereas most households (46 respondents) without agroforestry stated the opposite.

context.mitigation_mechanism: linear tree planting prevents soil erosion from floodwater

quote: [by] planting trees in a line, it prevents the soil from being swept away by water.
raw_name: prevent soil swept away by water
attribution: Male farmer in Burat, Kenya — household case study interview

context.mitigation_mechanism: deep root systems access subsoil water during drought
context.note: Quote refers to mango trees; mechanism is practice-technology level (deep root water access), not species-specific suitability

quote: Their roots travel far, trees are able to get water from deep down.
raw_name: roots travel far, get water from deep down

context.note: Table 4: Very important during drought: Burat 59.3%, Kinna 55.1%, Total 55.1%. Pearson chi2=23.07 P<0.01 for Burat

quote: Proportion (%) of survey respondents who listed agroforestry as being important for the household livelihood during drought, floods, and the 1997 El Niño flooding.

context.note: Table 4: Very important during floods: Burat 60.4%, Kinna 60%, Total 60.2%. Pearson chi2=8.05 P<0.05

quote: Proportion (%) of survey respondents who listed agroforestry as being important for the household livelihood during drought, floods, and the 1997 El Niño flooding.
Results from two communities in Isiolo County, Kenya indicate that agroforestry can and does build livelihood resilience to floods and drought.
Results from two communities in Isiolo County, Kenya indicate that agroforestry can and does build livelihood resilience to floods and drought.

context.note: Female farmer; trees maintain fruit production during drought when other crops fail

quote: We decided to plant trees because they do not dry up fast during times of drought... these times it helps us, the times when there is no rain, it helps us. If you water it a little bit, it produces fruit and you eat.
raw_name: trees do not dry up fast during drought

context.note: Table 3: top flood-period benefits were flood resistance (25 HH), windbreak (17 HH), food/income (27-28 HH), prevent erosion (6 HH), reduce water speed (9 HH)

quote: Tree species listed and the reasons given for their performance and benefits during floods and drought. Numbers indicate the number of household case study interviewees that named that specific tree and the corresponding specific benefit.
raw_name: flood resistant, prevent erosion, windbreak, reduce water speed

context.note: 74% planted trees deliberately for drought resilience; shows intentional adoption for climate hazard mitigation

quote: In both Burat and Kinna, 74% of respondents said that they had planted trees with drought in mind. In addition, 65% of respondents in Burat and 43% of respondents in Kinna said that they had planted trees with floods in mind.
raw_name: planted trees with drought in mind

relationship.note: Not statistically significant (p=0.13); high heterogeneity; random effects model

context.claim_basis_note: meta_analysis — SR pools 5 quasi-experimental impact evaluations
context.conditionality: high heterogeneity; positive effect concentrated in soil-fertility-replenishment practices in SSA

quote: agroforestry interventions overall may lead to a large, positive impact on yield (Hedge's g = 1.16 [−0.35, 2.67] (p = .13))

context.claim_basis_note: narrative_synthesis — SR narrating from cited primary studies
context.conditionality: severe soil fertility issues present; SSA context

relationship.note: Section 5.2.1; same estimate as abstract; high heterogeneity I2=98.99%

relationship.context: global maximum technical potential; croplands + grazing lands separately; Delphi-expert-calibrated tree cover x Cook-Patton carbon accumulation rates
relationship.conditionality: yield-safeguarded tree cover maximum; excludes shade-agroforestry; excludes Boreal; 30-year accumulation basis

quote: The global estimated maximum technical CO2 removal potential is split between croplands (1.86 PgCO2 yr− 1) and grazing lands (1.45 PgCO2 yr− 1), with large variances.
attribution: Sprenkle-Hyppolite et al. 2024 (primary)

relationship.context: combined cropland + grazing land; yield-safeguarded; maximum technical potential over 30 years

quote: increasing tree cover in 2.6 billion hectares of agricultural landscapes may remove up to 3.3 billion tons of CO2 per year – more than the global annual emissions from cars.

relationship.context: cropland; tropical vs temperate per-hectare rates; annual carbon accumulation

quote: tropical/subtropical biomes are likely to contain slightly over half (54%) of the global potential for TCA in croplands at 1.0 Pg CO2 yr- 1; with more than double the per hectare carbon accumulation rate at 2.8 Mg CO2 ha- 1 yr- 1 (SD = 0.45) in tropics vs. 1.2 Mg CO2 ha- 1 yr- 1 (SD = 0.22) in temperate.

relationship.context: grazing land (silvopastoral); tropical vs temperate per-hectare rates; annual carbon accumulation

quote: Tropical/subtropical biomes also contain an estimated 73% of the potential from TCA in grazing lands at 1.1 Pg CO2 yr- 1 with 3.5 times the per hectare carbon accumulation rate at 1.6 Mg CO2 ha- 1 yr- 1 (SD = 0.47) in tropics vs. 0.4 Mg CO2 ha- 1 yr- 1 (SD = 0.19) in temperate.

relationship.context: Sub-Saharan Africa croplands; highest per-hectare CO2 removal potential globally; Gabon, Benin, Republic of Congo, Togo

quote: Central and West Africa appear to contain hotspots of CO2 removal potential in croplands, with the top four countries globally in terms of potential per hectare of cropland being Gabon, Benin, Republic of Congo, and Togo.

relationship.context: Delphi expert elicitation constrained tree cover to levels that do not reduce long-term agricultural yields; yield safeguard is design condition, not observed outcome
relationship.conditionality: requires careful system design and monitoring; yield impacts vary by shade tolerance of crop and spatial arrangement of trees

quote: estimating opportunities to increase tree cover without reducing yields, therefore avoiding a major cost barrier to restoration: the opportunity cost of CO2 removal at the expense of agriculture yields.

relationship.context: forested biomes; mean maximum tree cover ~35%; shade-agroforestry level; shade-intolerant crops at risk of yield reduction
relationship.conditionality: applies where mean expert estimation approached shade-agroforestry levels (~35% canopy cover)

quote: these estimations must be applied with caution and close monitoring of agricultural yield impacts on shade-intolerant crops.

quote:
Based on total economic values costing US$873.07 
million37 and a social discount rate of 6 percent, Phase 1 is expected to have a net present value (NPV) of 
US$1,131.85 million and an economic internal rate of return (EIRR) of 25.8 percent.
attribution: World Bank FSRP Phase 1 (P178566) economic analysis section, para 112, 2022.

relationship.source_note: EIRR range when shadow price of carbon added; market SPC=27.9%, low SPC=40.4%, high SPC=72.9%, average SPC=52.1%; carbon sequestration 6.8–47.8% of benefits

Adding 
the SPC to the analysis increases the EIRR to 27.9, 40.4, 72.9 and 52.1 percent, using the World Bank’s market, 
low, and high and average SPCs, respectively, while the contribution of carbon sequestration varies from 6.8 to 
31.5 to 47.8 percent of overall benefits, respectively.

attribution: World Bank FSRP Phase 1 (P178566) economic analysis section, para 113, 2022.

context.note: General Kenya-wide claim within WB PAD economic analysis section; practice-level, not species-specific

quote: Practices such as agroforestry prevent land degradation and reduce losses of topsoil, thus inhibiting the escape of carbon into the atmosphere.

context.note: Sentence subject 'Forest and watershed protection' spans p.106; this continuation on p.107 lists flood/storm mitigation as CSA SLM benefit. Extraction confidence low because agroforestry not named explicitly in this sentence — inferred from SLM practices context.

quote: water flow regulation, including flood and storm protection, decreased rain water run-off (which could otherwise cause localized flooding, with possible adverse impact on fisheries and reservoirs), and water quantity and quality regulation; (iii) microclimate regulation

context.note: Footnote 20 on p.17; PAD citing Kenya NCCAP 2013–2017. Agroforestry listed alongside other CSA adaptation technologies for climate resilience in ASAL/semi-arid smallholder systems.

quote: NCCAP calls for increased investments in proven adaptation technologies such as agroforestry, conservation agriculture, integrated soil fertility management, drought-tolerant crops, water harvesting, drip irrigation, grazing management, fodder banks, and improved breeding.

counterpart funds (US$15.9 million equivalent), county governments (US$8.3 million), and

context.note: Project-level EIRR covering crop, livestock, agroforestry TIMPs; 264,000 ha targeted

quote:
The resulting economic net present value (NPV) is about 
US$304 million, and the economic internal rate of return (EIRR) is 16.7 percent. Sensitivity 
analyses demonstrate that the project can absorb substantial negative impacts and still generate 
an EIRR above the opportunity cost of capital; the analysis thus supports the public investment 
decision.

context.commodities: 11 commodities including cassava, sorghum, millet, banana, potato

quote:
Net present values of 
the net incremental benefits range fromUS$403 for millet to US$12,413 for honey. Benefit-cost 
ratios range from 1.40 for cassava-bean intercropping to 5.56 for honey.
raw_name: Net Present Value of incremental net benefits

quote:
Assuming that 60 percent of 
project farmers adopt at least one CSA TIMP, the net carbon balance over a period of 20 years is 
estimated to be –2,276,150 tCO2e (approximately –113,807 tCO2e per year). At a conservative 
carbon price (US$10/t), the value of the reduced GHG emissions under KCSAP is about 
US$22.76 million.

context.note: includes CIGs, VMGs, County investments, PPPs; 45% target female
CIG and VMG members only; county-level and PPP beneficiaries excluded from economic model

quote:
It is assumed that at the end of the project, 60 percent of beneficiaries targeted through 
CIGs and VMGs (approximately 108,900 beneficiary households) will have adopted at least one 
CSA TIMP.

The incremental net benefits over 20 years were US$794/acre, the benefit-cost 
(BC) ratio was 1.8, and switching values for benefits and costs were –44 percent and 79 percent.

The resulting annual net benefits are around US$152 per acre, with an NPV of incremental net benefits of US$403. 
The BC ratio of 1.45, and switching values for benefits of –31 percent and cost of 45 percent, 
attest to the robustness of the result.

The model assumes an increase in dry cassava yield (which is about 40 percent 
of fresh cassava) from 1.4 t/acre to 4 t/acre, leading to annual average net benefits of US$513, an 
NPV of incremental net benefits of US$2,523, and a BC ratio of 1.4. The switching values for 
benefits and costs are –29 percent and 40 percent, respectively.

These practices require 
approximately 20 percent more labor time and more production inputs but lead to an annual net 
benefit of US$6,485, an NPV of incremental net benefits over 20 years of US$5,803, and a BC 
ratio of 5.34. The switching values for benefits and costs are –81 percent and 434 percent, 
respectively.

context.note: Establishes agroforestry as explicit KCSAP target system

quote:
The KCSAP focuses primarily on: (i) improving water/soil management, especially 
within smallholder maize systems in the marginal rainfall zones—specifically, in smallholder 
mixed crop-livestock, crop-livestock-tree (agro-silvo-pastoral) production systems and in crop-
forest (agro-forestry) production systems

context.note: NPV positive across all scenarios tested; EIRR sensitive to land area and adoption

quote:
Based on the above assumptions, the analysis shows an NPV of US$304 million with an 
EIRR of 16.7 percent. Sensitivity analyses for key variables demonstrate the robustness of the 
results (Table A5.4). The NPV is positive for all proposed changes, and the EIRR is above 6 
percent. For some changes such as land area under cultivation, or number of participating 
beneficiaries, however, the EIRR becomes critically low.

relationship.note: Revised estimate combining IPCC Tier 1 defaults with MODIS tree-cover analysis; global agricultural land ~22.2 million km2

context.conditionality: applies where tree cover ≥10% on agricultural land; global estimate

quote: we estimated 45.3 PgC on agricultural land globally, with trees contributing >75%.
raw_name: global biomass carbon agricultural land revised estimate 45.3 PgC

relationship.note: Decadal trend 2000–2010; global agricultural land with tree cover

context.conditionality: net global trend; masks regional declines (Argentina, Sierra Leone, Myanmar)

quote: Between 2000 and 2010 tree cover increased by 3.7%, resulting in an increase of >2 PgC (or 4.6%) of biomass carbon. On average, globally, biomass carbon increased from 20.4 to 21.4 tC ha−1.
raw_name: biomass carbon increase 2000-2010 tC/ha global agricultural land

relationship.note: Compared to above-ground losses from tropical land use conversion of 0.6–1.2 PgC yr−1 (Houghton et al. cited)

context.conditionality: net global sequestration rate; comparison benchmark is deforestation losses 0.6–1.2 PgC yr−1

quote: Between 2000 and 2010, there was an increase of 0.2 PgC yr−1 of biomass carbon on agricultural land.
raw_name: annual biomass carbon increase rate 0.2 PgC/yr agricultural land

relationship.note: Global mean agricultural land including tree component; IPCC Tier 1 default ignores tree component

context.conditionality: reflects inclusion of tree component; IPCC baseline alone undercounts by >4x

quote: This gives a mean value of 20.4 tC ha−1 in 2000, and 21.4 tC ha−1 in 2010, which is more than four times larger than the IPCC Tier 1 global estimate of 5 tC ha−1.
raw_name: per-hectare biomass carbon 20.4 tC/ha vs IPCC 5 tC/ha

context.conditionality: regional variation — dry arid areas have less potential; humid regions show divergence from climate-expected levels in some cases

quote: in many regions there is still potential for increasing biomass carbon on agricultural land
raw_name: potential for increasing biomass carbon on agricultural land — regional scope statement

context.conditionality: land with ≥10% tree cover on agricultural area in 2010 (FAO forest definition threshold)
context.note: From figure caption; co-benefit framing — livelihoods context, not a direct carbon claim

quote: these land-use types represent over 1 billion hectares of land and provide subsistence to more than 900 million people
raw_name: agroforestry land >1 billion ha; 900 million people dependent — figure 1 caption

context.note: Framing statement — authors contest this view; the paper argues biomass C is underestimated, not that SOC is unimportant

quote: the biomass carbon pool on agricultural land is seen as arguably negligible compared to the soil organic carbon (SOC) pool
raw_name: biomass carbon vs SOC pool framing statement
attribution: Lal 2004 (Science 304:1623) and Banwart et al. (SCOPE) cited as refs 20–21

context.conditionality: long-term tree retention on agricultural land required

quote: Trees are relatively permanent, and their biomass contributes to building up SOC over the longer term
attribution: Cited refs 10, 11 in paper (general agroforestry carbon literature)

context.conditionality: tree cover presence in agroforestry systems; magnitude of SOC effect not quantified in this paper
context.note: Authors frame SOC as corollary benefit beyond the biomass carbon scope of this paper

quote: facilitation of increased SOC by tree cover, as found within many agroforestry systems, should be highlighted as an important corollary benefit
